Facts.text: Philips of Zwabia, Prince of Hohenstaufen, crowned King of Germany and King of the Romans
William Cecil appoints himself English Secretary of State
The first public library in the Americas is established when Bishop Palafox y Mendoza donates his personal library of 5,000 volumes to Tridentine colleges in Puebla, Mexico
Decree issued in Paderborn, Prussia, allows for the annual search of all Jewish homes for stolen or "doubtful" goods
The first Continental Congress, a meeting of delegates from 12 of the 13 British colonies that later become the United States, convenes at Carpenters' Hall in Philadelphia
Twelve of the thirteen American colonies adopt a trade embargo against Great Britain at the First Continental Congress in Carpenters' Hall, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
With the meeting of the Continental Congress, Philadelphia becomes the first capital of the United States
USA and Algiers sign peace treaty
General Salicetti orders equal rights for the Jews of Bologna, Italy
New annual military conscription law goes into effect in France
Malta surrenders to the British after they blockade French troops
Battle of Masurian Lakes: Germans chase Russians out of East Prussia (ends September 15, 1814)
Central Museum opens in Utrecht, Netherlands
First Opium War begins between the British Empire and the Qing dynasty of China
Iron ore is discovered in Minnesota's Mesabi Mountains
Bread revolt in Mobile, Alabama
Achille François Bazaine becomes Marshal of France
British, French, and Dutch fleets attack Japan in the Shimonoseki Straits
German archaeologist Carl Mauch is the first European to explore the ruins of the medieval Shona city of Great Zimbabwe, the largest archaeological site in Sub-Saharan Africa
Southern Blacks, led by Pap Singleton, settle in Kansas
American Arctic explorer George Washington De Long on board the Jeannette becomes trapped with his crew in pack ice during an attempt to reach the North Pole
10,000 workers march in the first Labor Day parade in New York City
First gasoline pump is delivered to a gasoline dealer in Fort Wayne, Indiana
Gas lamp at Theatre Royal in Exeter catches fire, killing about 200 people
German Christine Hardt patents the first modern brassiere
George Washington Murray from South Carolina is elected to Congress
France proclaims a protectorate over Chad
National Association of Professional Baseball Leagues, better known as Minor League Baseball, is formed at the Leland Hotel in Chicago
Fifty prominent men meet in Sydney's Australia Hotel to found the National Defence League, fueled by fear of Japan after its victory over Russia
Lillian Mortimer's play "No Mother to Guide Her" premieres in Detroit

Saint Louis University quarterback Bradbury Robinson throws the first legal forward pass in the history of American football for a touchdown to Jack Schneider at Carroll College in Waukesha, Wisconsin; Saint Louis wins 22-0
Brooklyn Superbas pitcher Nap Rucker no-hits the Boston Braves 6-0 at Washington Park, Brooklyn
Philadelphia Athletics player Jack Coombs begins a record streak of 53 consecutive shutout innings
Phillies and Braves tie the record of only one run in a doubleheader; Phillies win the first game 1-0, then a scoreless tie into the 10th
French headquarters move to Châtillon-sur-Seine
Great Britain, France, Belgium, and Russia sign the Pact of London
Proclamation prohibits Canadian mint from issuing gold coins
Anti-war conference in Zimmerwald, Switzerland
Decree of the Council of People's Commissars "On Red Terror" is published in Russia
Flyweights Gene LaRue and Kid Pancho knock each other out simultaneously
112°F (44°C) in Centerville, Alabama (state record)
French Upper Volta is broken apart between Ivory Coast, French Sudan, and Niger
Spanish Civil War: Llanes falls
American sailor Charles J. French (22) swims for over six hours in waters near Guadalcanal while towing a life raft with fifteen survivors from his US Navy ship, which is sunk by Japanese gunfire
Battle of Alam Halfa ends
British and US bomb Le Havre and Bremen
The US airlands at Nadzab, New Guinea
"Mad Tuesday" sees 65,000 Dutch Nazi collaborators flee to Germany
Allies liberate Brussels, Belgium
Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands sign a customs union treaty
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchilltravels to Scotland
Dutch Armed Forces forms under Prince Bernhard
Five resistance fighters executed in Terneuzen, Netherlands
In France, Robert Schuman becomes President of the Council while being Foreign Minister; as such, he is the negotiator of the major treaties at the end of World War II
38.7 inches (98.3 cm) of rainfall over several days at Yankeetown, Florida (state record)
General Carlos Ibáñez is elected president of Chile
First privately operated atomic reactor becomes operational in Raleigh, North Carolina
US gives Persian Premier Fazlollah Zahedi $45 million in aid
Dutch Super Constellation crashes at Shannon, killing 28
Dodger Don Newcombe hits a National League pitcher record seventh home run of the season
Dutch magician Fred Kaps wins his second FISM World Championship
WKRG TV channel 5 in Mobile, AL (CBS) begins broadcasting
WTTW TV channel 11 in Chicago, IL (PBS) begins broadcasting
20 die in a train crash in Springer, New Mexico
First color video recording on magnetic tape is presented in Charlotte, North Carolina
WKPC TV channel 15 in Louisville, Kentucky (PBS) begins broadcasting
Washington Senators player Jim Lemon becomes the seventh player to achieve six RBIs in an inning (third)
US PresidentJohn F. Kennedysigns a law against hijacking, instituting the death penalty
USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeastern Kazakhstan
Cubs' Ken Hubbs sets second base record for consecutive errorless games at 78 and consecutive errorless chances at 418; he errors in the fourth
WRLK TV channel 35 in Columbia, SC (PBS) begins broadcasting
Hurricane Beulah kills 54 in the Caribbean, Mexico, and Texas
KMEG TV channel 14 in Sioux City, IA (CBS) begins broadcasting
WEBA TV channel 14 in Allendale, SC (PBS) begins broadcasting
The USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeastern Kazakhstan
Frente Obrero wins Dutch Antilles' national elections
Estimated 15 cm (6 inches) of rainfall in Bug Point, Utah (state record)
Astros pitcher J.R. Richard debuts, striking out 15 Giants in a 5-3 win
New York Mets' Don Hahn hits his first inside-the-park home run at Phillies Vet
Chemical spill with fog sickens hundreds in Meuse Valley, Belgium
Eleven Israeli athletes are taken hostage and later killed by the Palestinian group Black September at the Munich Olympics
Conference of less developed countries approves forming "producers' associations" and calls for the withdrawal of Israeli forces from occupied Arab lands
West Indies lose by one wicket to England in the first one-day cricket international
Portugal Prime Minister Vasco Gonçalves is dismissed
Wings release their single "Letting Go"
Cleveland Indians stage first "I Hate the Yankee Hanky Night," inspired by local radio personality Pete Franklin; home team sweeps a doubleheader over New York at Cleveland Municipal Stadium
Jerry Lewis's 12th Muscular Dystrophy telethon is held
NASA launches Voyager 1 on a mission to initially fly by Jupiter and Saturn, later including Uranus and Neptune, and in 2012, it becomes the first human-made object to leave the solar system
Red Army Faction kidnaps and subsequently murders West German business executive Hanns Martin Schleyer
Canada puts its first gold bullion coin on sale
Iranian army occupies Piranshahr
Oakland A's pitcher Matt Keough beats the Milwaukee Brewers 6-1 for his first win after 14 straight losses, ending 1978 with 18 defeats, one shy of the MLB record of 19
Edward Gierek resigns under pressure from his position as Poland's Communist Party leader
World's longest road tunnel, St. Gotthard in the Swiss Alps, opens
Eddie Hill sets a propeller-driven boat water speed record of 229 mph (368.54 km/h)
US Men's Amateur Golf Championship, The Country Club: Jay Sigel wins 8 and 7 over David Tolley
Eighth NASA Space Shuttle Mission: Challenger 3 lands at Edwards AFB
Elmer Trettr sets the record for the highest terminal velocity at 201.34 mph (324.03 km/h)
Jerry Lewis's 18th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $30,691,627
NASA's Space Shuttle Discovery completes its first spaceflight, STS-41-D, with a landing at Edwards Air Force Base, California
Hijacking of aircraft Pan Am 73 at Karachi airport, Pakistan; 20 passengers are killed
NASA awards study contracts to five aerospace firms
NASA launches DoD-1, a classified Department of Defense satellite
Carlton Fisk hits his 300th career home run off Danny Jackson
Jerry Lewis's 23rd Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $41,132,113
Deborah Norville becomes the news anchor of the Today Show
Dan O'Brien sets a world record in the decathlon with 8891 points
F. Murray Abrahamis released from hospital after a car accident
Noureddine Morceli runs a world record mile in 3:44.39
Jerry Lewis's 29th Muscular Dystrophy telethon raises $47,100,000
Jingyi Le swims a world record in the 100 m women's freestyle in 54.01 seconds
SF wide receiverJerry Ricecatches 2 touchdown passes and runs for another score in the 49ers' 44-14 rout of the Raiders, surpassing Jim Brown as the NFL's career TD leader with 127
Following US cruise missile strikes on Iraq, crude oil prices rise as the market speculates about when Iraq will begin exporting oil under UN Resolution 986
Athens in Greece is selected to host the 2004 Olympics
Orioles beat Yankees 13-9 in the longest nine-inning game
The Haverstraw-Ossining Ferry makes its maiden voyage
Mandala Airlines Flight 91 crashes into a heavily populated residential area seconds after taking off from Medan in Sumatra, Indonesia, killing 100 people on board and 49 people on the ground
Three terrorists suspected to be part of Al-Qaeda are arrested in Germany after allegedly planning attacks on both Frankfurt International Airport and US military installations
Haiti's government reports a death toll of at least 529 people as a result of flooding caused by Tropical Storm Hanna around the northern port city of Gonaïves
Denmark celebrates the first national Flag Day in memory of the fallen Danes in international operations since 1948
The Staffordshire Hoard, the largest Anglo-Saxon gold and silver metalwork hoard ever found with over 4,600 items, is discovered on farmland near Hammerwich, England
25 people are killed and 4 wounded after an ammunition store explodes in Afyon, Turkey
54 people are killed and 50 injured after a firecracker factory explodes in Tamil Nadu
Austerity measures require Greece to increase its maximum working days to six per week
World Health Organization estimates 1,900 people have died from the Ebola virus out of 3,500 infected in Guinea, Liberia, Nigeria, Senegal, and Sierra Leone
US health officials confirm a salmonella outbreak linked to cucumbers from Mexico is responsible for one death and for making hundreds sick
Barry Callebaut announces a fourth type of chocolate, "Ruby," made from the Ruby cocoa bean
Brenda Hale, Baroness Hale of Richmond, becomes the first woman President of the UK Supreme Court
Togo's government shuts down the internet for a week to suppress government opposition
Anonymous senior White House official opinion piece "I Am Part of the Resistance Inside the Trump Administration" published by The New York Times
Erramatti Mangamma becomes the world's oldest living mother after giving birth to twins at age 74 in Hyderabad, India
New theory suggests the Loch Ness Monster may be a giant eel after DNA study reveals no plesiosaur or sturgeon DNA found
South African women march on Parliament to protest violence against women after a month when 30 were killed by their spouses
More than 50 are arrested as Portland, Oregon, observes 100 days of protests against racism and police brutality
Coup by soldiers in Guinea led by Colonel Doumbouya deposes President Alpha Condé and his government, citing rampant corruption
Tour Championship, Men's Golf, East Lake GC, GA: Patrick Cantlay claims the highest prize in golf ($15 million) with a one-stroke win over Jon Rahm; runner-up Rahm earns $5 million
6.6-magnitude earthquake strikes Chinese city of Chengdu while it is under lockdown, killing 65 people
Ringo Starrresumes his All-Starr Band tour after two musicians recover from COVID-19
American singer Joe Jonas (34) files for divorce from English actress Sophie Turner (27) in Miami, Florida, after 4 years of marriage
An extratropical cyclone in Brazil's southern state of Rio Grande do Sul claims at least 31 lives as extensive flooding affects 60 cities
Birmingham City Council, the largest local authority in Europe, effectively declares itself bankrupt after stopping all nonessential spending
Former Proud Boys chairman Enrique Tarrio is sentenced to 22 years in prison, the longest so far, for seditious conspiracy in the January 6 attack on the US Capitol
China confirms it is ending its foreign adoption program following a suspension that began with the COVID-19 pandemic in January 2020
Kuini Ngawai Hono i te Po (27) is named the new Māori Monarch as her father, King Tuheitia, is buried at Tūrangawaewae Marae, New Zealand
US President Donald Trump deploys 10 F-35 fighter jets to Puerto Rico to take part in military action against drug cartels in the Caribbean region
Second Battle of Tehuacingo, Mexico:Hernán Cortés' Spanish conquistadors fight the Tlaxcalans
Duke of Parma Alexander Farnese's army forcesHenry IV of Franceto raise the siege of Paris
Dutch fleet commanderCornelis de Houtmanis taken hostage in Java and is later ransomed
Bishop Richelieu appointed Cardinal under French KingLouis XIII
Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange conquers Sas van Gent and absorbs it into the Dutch Republic
Nicolas Fouquet, French Superintendent of Finances underLouis XIV, is arrested for maladministration of state funds; he dies in 1680, never seeing freedom again
Russian TsarPeter the Greatimposes a tax on beards
Britain's KingGeorge Iissues the Proclamation "For Suppressing Pirates in the West Indies," granting pirates who surrender a pardon (reissued 1718)
French Revolution: the "Reign of Terror" begins
French KingLouis XVIIIdissolves the unpopular parliament, Chambre introuvable (Unobtainable Chamber)
Sam Houstonelected President of the Republic of Texas
Confederate GeneralRobert E. Leecrosses the Potomac and enters Maryland
Treaty of Portsmouth is signed concluding the Russo-Japanese War; US PresidentTheodore Rooseveltreceives the Nobel Peace Prize for his role as mediator
KingEdward VIIof Great Britain meets Russia's Foreign Minister Alexander Izvolsky in an attempt to strengthen Russia's relationship with Britain

US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonorders the US Navy to make its wireless stations accessible for any transatlantic communications, even to German diplomats sending coded messages, leading to the interception of the Zimmermann telegram and helping bring the US into the war
TsarNicholas II, distressed by increasing Russian losses, assumes personal command of his nation's military forces, a symbolic act devastating for his leadership
"Intolerance," a silent film directed byD. W. Griffithand starring Vera Lewis and Ralph Lewis, is released
Walter Johnsonsets MLB strikeout record at 2,287
French premierAristide Briandrequests a United States of Europe
American pilotWiley Postdiscovers the jet stream while flying at high altitude near Chicago, Illinois
Franklin D. Rooseveltdeclares US neutrality at the start of WWII in Europe
J. B. M. Hertzogresigns as South African Prime Minister after losing a vote in Parliament on neutrality in World War II
New Zealand Prime MinisterMichael Joseph Savagedeclares New Zealand's support for Britain in the war with Germany; Savage famously told the nation 'where she goes, we go. Where she stands, we stand'

Amon Göth, former head of Kraków-Płaszów concentration camp, is found guilty of imprisonment, torture, and extermination of individuals and groups of people in the first conviction of homicide at a war crimes court

First performance of Gerald Finzi's setting ofWilliam Wordsworth's "Intimations of Immortality" at the Three Choirs Festival in Gloucester Cathedral, England; Eric Greene as solo tenor with Herbert Sumsion conducting the orchestra and choir
"On the Road" byJack Kerouacis published by Viking Press in New York
Cuban dictatorFulgencio Batistabombs an uprising in Cienfuegos
Yugoslavia bans dissidentMilovan Djilas' book "The New Class: An Analysis of the Communist System"
"The Huckleberry Hound Show" byHanna-Barberafeaturing Yogi Bear premieres on US TV
The novel "Doctor Zhivago" byBoris Pasternakis published in the US
A. J. Foytwins the first of 67 Indy car victories at Du Quoin State Fairgrounds in Illinois
Cassius Clay [Muhammad Ali] defeats three-time European champion Zbigniew Pietrzykowski of Poland by unanimous points decision to win the Olympic light heavyweight boxing gold medal at the Rome Games
Future world middleweight boxing championNino Benvenutiof Italy defeats Yuri Radonyak of the Soviet Union to win the welterweight gold medal at the Rome Olympics
PoetLéopold Sédar Senghoris elected as the first President of Senegal
President of CongoJoseph Kasa-Vubufires Prime MinisterPatrice Lumumba
America begins underground nuclear testing
Jerry Lewis's first Muscular Dystrophy Labor Day telethon raises $1 million
First assassination attempt on US PresidentGerald Fordby Lynette "Squeaky" Fromme in Sacramento, California
Jim Henson's "The Muppet Show" premieres on television with Mia Farrow as the guest star
Anwar Sadat,Menachem Begin, andJimmy Carterbegin the Egypt-Israel peace conference at Camp David
Earl ofMountbatten's ceremonial funeral is held in Westminster Abbey, London
Philip Glass and Constance DeJong's opera "Satyagraha," loosely based on the life ofMahatma Gandhi, premieres at the Schouwburg in Rotterdam, Netherlands
Daytime talk show "TheJoan RiversShow," hosted by Joan Rivers, premieres on US TV
Iraqi PresidentSaddam Husseinurges Arabs to rise against the West
Nelson Mandelais chosen as president of the African National Congress
Cal Ripken Jr. ties Gehrig's record of playing in 2,130 consecutive games
John Cage's musical composition "As Slow as Possible", intended to be played for 639 years, begins at St. Burchardi Church, Halberstadt, Germany
UK Prime MinisterTheresa Mayconfirms in Parliament that two Russian military intelligence officers undertake a Novichok nerve agent attack, prompting international condemnation
Stewart Copeland's opera "Electric Saint" about the life ofNikola Tesla, with libretto by Jonathan Moore, premieres at the Deutsches Nationaltheater in Weimar, Germany
UK's ruling Conservative party appointsLiz Trussas their next leader and Prime Minister, replacing scandal-riddenBoris Johnson
PresidentXi Jinpinghosts a China-Africa summit with 50 African delegates focusing on developing green energy
Firebreaks finally bring the Great Fire of London under control, leaving 13,200 houses destroyed and eight dead

A French fleet of 24 ships under Comte de Grasse defeats British forces under Admiral Thomas Graves andSamuel Hoodat the Battle of the Chesapeake (Battle of the Virginia Capes) and traps General Lord Charles Cornwallis during the American Revolutionary War
Hurricane Irma becomes one of the most powerful hurricanes ever recorded in the Atlantic Basin region with winds of 185 mph (280 km/h)
Louis VIII [Coeur-de-Lion], King of France (1223-26), born in Paris (d. 1226)
Peter IV, King of Aragon (1336-87), born in Perpignan, France (d. 1387)
Date Masamune, Renowned Samurai and Daimyo in Japan, born in Yonezawa, Yamagata, Japan (d. 1636)
Tommaso Campanella, Italian theologian, philosopher, and poet, born in Stignano, Kingdom of Sicily (d. 1639)
Loreto Vittori, Italian composer and mezzo-soprano singer, baptized in Spoleto, Umbria, Papal States (d. 1670)
Juan Andrés Coloma, Spanish noble, born in Elda, Spain (d. 1694)
Gillis Schey, Dutch admiral during the Nine Years War, born in Arnhem (d. 1703)
Louise de Kérouaille, Duchess of Portsmouth, French mistress to British King Charles II, born near Best, Brittany (d. 1734)
Gottfried Arnold, German theologist, historian and songwriter, born in Annaberg-Buchholz, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1714)
Giovanni Gerolamo Saccheri, Italian mathematician, born in Sanremo, Italy (d. 1733)
František Václav Míča, Moravian-Czech conductor and composer, born in Třebíč, Margraviate of Moravia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1744)
Carl Gustaf Tessin, Swedish politician, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1770)
Frederick Christian, Elector of Saxony, born in Dresden Castle, Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1763)
Jean-Étienne Montucla, French mathematician, born in Lyon, France (d. 1799)
Jean-Benjamin de La Borde, French composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1794)
Johann Christian Bach (English Bach), German composer, 11th son of Johann Sebastian Bach, born in Leipzig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (1782)
Johann Friedrich Gottlieb Beckmann, German composer, born in Celle, Electorate of Hanover, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1792)
Robert Fergusson, Scottish poet (Scottish poems), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1774)
Henry of Stolberg-Wernigerode, German ruler of the County of Wernigerode (1824-54), born in Wernigerode Castle, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1854)

Caspar David Friedrich, German painter, chiefly of landscapes with
contemplative figures in the Romantic style, born in Greifswald, Swedish Pomerania (now Germany) (d.  1840)
Juan Martín Díez, El Empecinado (The Undaunted), Spanish guerrillero, born in Valladolid, Spain (d. 1825)
François Sulpice Beudant, French mineralogist and geologist, born in Paris (d. 1850)
George Macfarren, British playwright, born in London, England (d. 1843)
Giacomo Meyerbeer, German opera composer (Gott und die Natur - God and Nature), born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1864)
Alexis-Charles-Maximilien Thibault, French composer, born in Nantes, France (d. 1853)
Pierre-Armand Dufrénoy, French geologist and mineralogist, born in Sevran, France (d. 1857)
Christophe Léon Louis Juchault de Lamoricière, French general, born in Nantes, France (d. 1865)
Manuel Montt Torres, President of Chile (851-61), born in Petorca, Chile (d. 1880)
Karl Wilhelm, German composer, born in Schmalkalden, Electorate of Hesse, German Confederation (d. 1873)
Tyree Harris Bell, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Covington, Kentucky (d. 1902)
Aleksei K. Tolstoi, Russian poet and writer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1875) [NS]
John Wisden, English cricket all-rounder (launched Wisden Cricketers' Almanack 1864; Sussex CCC, Kent CCC, Middlesex CCC), born in Brighton, England (d. 1884)
Goffredo Mameli, Italian poet and writer, born in Genoa, Italy (d. 1849)
Justiniano Borgoño, President of Peru (1894), born in Trujillo, Peru (d. 1921)
Louis Bouwmeester, Dutch actor (Shakespeare), born in Middelharnis, Netherlands (d. 1925)
John W Cromwell, American lawyer and civil right activist (American Negro Academy), born in Portsmouth, Virginia (d. 1927)
Manuel Giro, Spanish composer, born in Lérida, Catalonia, Spain (d. 1916)
Lester A. Pelton, American inventor (water wheel for hydroelectricity), born in Vermilion, Ohio (d. 1908)[1]
Horace Rice, Australian tennis player (Australasian C'ship 1907), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1950)
Napoleon "Nap" Lajoie, American Baseball HOF second baseman (Triple Crown 1901; AL batting champion 1901–04, 10; Philadelphia A's, Cleveland Naps), born in Woonsocket, Rhode Island (d. 1959)
Abdelaziz Thâalbi, Tunisian politician, born in Tunis, Tunisia (d. 1944)
Wilhelm Ritter von Leeb, German field marshal, born in Landsberg am Lech, Germany (d. 1956)
Albert Roelofs, Dutch painter and etcher, born in Schaarbeek, Brussels, Belgium (d. 1920)
Ben Simpson, Canadian Football HOF running back, placekicker (Queen's University, Hamilton Tigers), born in Peterborough, Ontario (d. 1964)
Otto Bauer, Austrian Social Democrat, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1938)
Mel Sheppard, American athlete (Olympic gold 800m, 1,500m, medley relay 1908; 4x400 metre relay 1912), born in Deptford Township, New Jersey (d. 1942)
Otto Erich Deutsch, Austrian musicologist (Schubert-Brevier), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1967)
John Raedecker, Dutch sculptor (National monument on the Dam), born in Amsterdam (d. 1956)
Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan, Indian philosopher and leader, Second President of India (1962-67), born in Thiruttani, British India (d. 1975)
Joseph Szigeti, Hungarian violinist (Violinist Notebook 1933), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1973)
Arthur C. Nielsen, American businessman and market researcher (TV's Nielsen's Ratings), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1980)
Doris Kenyon, American silent screen actress (Alexander Hamilton), born in Syracuse, New York (d. 1979)
Luella Gear, American actress (Joe & Mabel), born in New York City (d. 1980)
Morris Carnovsky, American actor (Dead Reckoning), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1992)
Ebbe Hamerik, Danish opera composer (Stepan; Marie Grubbe), born in Frederiksberg, Denmark (d. 1951)
Florence Eldridge [McKechnie], American Broadway stage and screen actress (Long Day's Journey Into Night; The Swan; Inherit The Wind), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1988)
Mieczyslaw Kolinski, Polish-Canadian composer (Encounterpoint), educator, and ethnomusicologist, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1981)
Darryl F. Zanuck, American film producer and President (20th Century Fox), born in Wahoo, Nebraska (d. 1979)
Gloria Holden, British actress (The Life of Émile Zola, Dracula's Daughter), born in London, England (d. 1991)
Arthur Koestler, Hungarian-British writer (Arrow in Blue), born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1983)
Justiniano Montano, Filipino politician, born in Tanza, Cavite, Philippines (d. 2005)
Peter Mieg, Swiss composer, born in Lenzburg, Switzerland (d. 1990)
Sunnyland Slim [Albert Luandrew], American blues pianist, born in Vance, Mississippi (d. 1995)
Cecilia Seghizzi, Italian composer, born in Gotizia, Austria-Hungary (d. 2019)
Joaquin Maria Nin-Culmell, Cuban-Spanish composer, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2004)
Archie Jackson, Australian cricket batsman (8 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 164; NSWCA), born in Rutherglen, Scotland (d. 1933)
Hans Carste, German stage, radio, and film composer ("Du spielst 'ne tolle Rolle" aka "Those Lazy-Hazy-Crazy Days of Summer"), and orchestra leader, born in Frankenthal, Germany (d. 1971)
Phiroz Palia, Indian cricket batsman (2 Tests; United Provinces), born in Mumbai, India (d. 1981)
Frank Thomas, American animator, born in Fresno, California (d. 2004)
Kristina Söderbaum, German actress and photographer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 2001)
Conny Stuart [Cornelia van Meygard], Dutch cabaret performer, and actress, born in Wijhe, Netherlands (d. 2010)
Kathleen Burke, American actress (Island of Lost Souls), born in Hammond, Indiana (d. 1980)
Gail Kubik, American composer (Gerald McBoing Boing), born in South Coffeyville, Oklahoma (d. 1984)
Nicanor Parra, Chilean poet, mathematician, and physicist (Defense of Violeta Parra), born in San Fabián de Alico, Chile (d. 2018)
Stuart Freeborn, English motion picture make-up artist (Star Wars: Yoda, Jabba the Hut), born in London, England (d. 2013)
Frank Shuster, Canadian comedian (Wayne and Shuster), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2002)
Frank Yerby, American novelist (The Foxes of Harrow), born in Augusta, Georgia (d. 1991)
Art Rupe [Goldberg], American gospel, R&B, and early rock record producer and label executive (Specialty Records - Little Richard, Sam Cooke, Lloyd Price), born in Greensburg, Pennsylvania (d. 2022)
Jack Buetel, American actress (Outlaw, Half Breed), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1989)
Luis Alcoriza, Mexican screenwriter, film director, and actor, born in Badajoz, Mexico (d. 1992)
Fons Rademakers, Dutch director (Assault) and actor (Daughter of Darkness), born in Roosendaal, Netherlands (d. 2007)
Margaretha D. Ferguson-Wigerink, Dutch author (Anna & her Father), born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Peter Racine Fricker, British composer, born in London (d. 1990)
Jack Valenti, American political advisor and film executive, born in Houston, Texas (d. 2007)
Bob Cato, American photographer, graphic artist and Grammy Award-winning album cover designer (Columbia Records; United Artists), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1999)
Krystyna Moszumanska-Nazar, Polish pianist, composer (Polish Madonnas; Fantasy for Marimba), and educator, born in Lwów, Poland (d. 2008)
Jos Vandeloo, Flemish journalist and playwright (Cola Drinkers), born in Zonhoven, Belgium (d. 2015)
Justin Kaplan, American editor and biographer (Mr. Clemens and Mark Twain - Pulitzer Prize, 1967), born in New York City (d. 2014)
Carmen Petra-Basacopol, Romanian composer and educator (Bucharest Conservatory, 1962-2003), born in Sibiu, Transylvania, Romania (d. 2023)
Albert Mangelsdorff, German free-jazz trombonist, and multiphonics innovator, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 2005)
Lena McLin (née Johnson), American music teacher, composer (Oh Freedom), and Baptist pastor, born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 2023)
Andrian Nikolayev, Soviet cosmonaut (Vostok III, Soyuz 9), born in Shorshely, Russia (d. 2004)
Ildefonso P. Santos Jr., Filipino landscape architect (National Artist of the Philippines in Architecture, 2006), born in Malabon, Philippines (d. 2014)
Moshe Mizrahi, Israeli film director (Madame Rosa), born in Alexandria, Egypt (d. 2018)
Carol Lawrence, American stage and screen actress (West Side Story), and singer, born in Melrose Park, Illinois
Francisco Javier Errázuriz Ossa, Chilean catholic archbishop, born in Santiago, Chile
Vincent McDermott, American composer, born in Atlantic City, New Jersey (d. 2016)
Helen Gifford, Australian composer (Of Old Angkor; Music for the Adonia; Choral Scenes: The Western Front, World War I), born in Hawthorn, Melbourne, Australia
Johnny Briggs, English actor (Mike Baldwin on Coronation Street), born in London, England (d. 2021)
Mikhail Ivanovich Lisun, Russian cosmonaut, born in Gruzkoye, Ukraine (d. 2012)
Werner Erhard, American author and lecturer (founded EST), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Alcee Hastings, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Florida), born in Altamonte Springs, Florida (d. 2021)
Bill Mazeroski, American Baseball HOF 2nd baseman (World Series 1960, 71 Pittsburgh Pirates; 10 × MLB All-Star; 8 × Gold Glove Award), born in Wheeling, West Virginia
Cornelius Boyson, American blues bassist, born in Tunica, Mississippi (d. 1994)
John Danforth, American politician and diplomat (Senator-R-Missouri, 1976-95), born in St. Louis, Missouri
Jonathan Kozol, American writer and sociologist, born in Boston, Massachusetts
